post_mortem: The Mets built a 5-0 lead and the thesis was playing out — then a once-in-a-generation play erased it. With the bases loaded in the 2nd, McLean's sweeper to James Wood glanced off debutant Nick Morabito's arm in left field and caromed into center, allowing Wood to score an inside-the-park grand slam — only the 9th such play since 1994. The Nationals then added three more in the 3rd on a homer, a passed ball, and a sac fly to go up 7-5. McLean's final line (9 R, 6 ER, 5.2 IP) was a career-worst, but three of those runs were unearned and the decisive blow was a freak defensive misplay on a ball that had no business leaving the infield. The pick was correctly reasoned; a ball bouncing off a rookie's arm in the 2nd inning is not a model failure.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule; the inside-the-park grand slam is a ~0.1% frequency event and not a repeatable risk factor to model against.

post_mortem: The thesis correctly identified Rogers as a liability but fatally misread HOW he damages you. The pick's own 'case against' warned Rogers hurts via solo shots and walks — not clusters of singles — yet Rogers sent 10 batters to the plate in a five-run second inning, exactly the station-to-station cluster damage the model said was unlikely. Tampa Bay then batted around again in a four-run sixth, capped by Caminero's three-run homer, pushing the total to 22 runs (TB 16, BAL 6) — nearly triple the 7.5 line. McClanahan did his job (held Baltimore scoreless into the third, extending his streak to 23⅔ innings), but the under was already dead by the end of the second inning on Rogers alone. The model's core error: it discounted the 'big inning via traffic' scenario for Rogers despite his 1.54 WHIP screaming exactly that risk.
candidate_rule: When a starter carries a WHIP above 1.45, do not lean Under regardless of the opposing ace — high-WHIP pitchers generate multi-run innings via baserunner accumulation at a rate that overwhelms even elite run suppression on the other side; flag any Under pick where one starter's WHIP exceeds 1.40 for manual review. (N=1, insufficient to establish rule — monitor for pattern.)
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One blowup from a high-WHIP starter does not validate a blanket filter — watch for recurrence across 10+ similar setups before codifying.

post_mortem: This game was a double-overtime Western Conference Finals opener — OKC was not blown out. Per CBS Sports, the Spurs were up 10 with 9:10 left in regulation, OKC clawed back, and the lead changed hands twice with the game tied three times in under two minutes before the clock ran out on regulation. Wembanyama (41 pts, 24 reb) and Harper (24 pts, 7 steals) took over in double OT to seal it — the Thunder -6.5 cover was alive deep into the fourth quarter and was ultimately killed by two overtime periods, not by a bad thesis.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. The pick was correctly reasoned — OKC led or was competitive through regulation — and was undone by a specific, low-probability double-OT outcome, not a structural model failure.

post_mortem: The thesis was predicated on Giolito being the weak link — he wasn't. Giolito allowed one hit through five innings in a strong debut. The loss came from Kirby, who was torched for five runs in the sixth: Sheets hit two homers off him (including a hanging changeup in the 6th), Merrill added an RBI double, and France's two-out single made it 7-0. Kirby entered the game 0-2 with a 10.38 ERA in two prior starts vs. the Padres — a red flag the pick acknowledged but underweighted, calling it a 1-unit play instead of a pass.
candidate_rule: When the starting pitcher on the 'safe' side of an under has a documented ERA above 8.00 in multiple prior starts against the opposing lineup, treat that as a disqualifying factor for the under regardless of park factors — but validate against a larger sample before hardcoding.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule. One loss where Kirby's Padres-specific splits were the deciding factor is noise; need N≥5 similar setups to confirm the pattern.

post_mortem: Through nine innings this game was exactly what the thesis promised: a 2-2 tie, both starters doing their jobs (Martinez held Miami to 1 run through 7, Alcantara surrendered 7 hits in 6 innings but kept it close), and the under alive with room to spare. Then the 10th inning arrived and the Rays' bullpen — specifically Hunter Bigge — imploded catastrophically, surrendering 8 runs on 6 hits including a Liam Hicks two-run single and a Javier Sanoja double that plated three, turning a 2-2 game into a 10-2 final before Tampa added 3 garbage runs of their own. The under was breathing comfortably at 4 total runs through 9; a single bullpen meltdown in extras buried it.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient to establish rule — this is a clean VARIANCE loss, not a modeling failure requiring a corrective rule.

post_mortem: The thesis was structurally sound — two starters with low walk rates, a HR-suppressing dome, and a flat market line all pointed toward a low-event game. A 10-5 final (15 runs) represents a significant blowup that the underlying profile didn't predict, likely driven by a multi-run inning or early starter exit — exactly the tail risk flagged in the case against. The data was accurate and the reasoning was coherent; the outcome was simply in the bad tail.
sample_size_warning: N=1, insufficient — one high-scoring outlier in a dome game does not invalidate dome suppression factors or low-walk-rate pitcher theses.
